title: Hardware Capability Validation and Environment Setup
description: Validate hardware PMU capabilities and measure PMU profiling overhead BEFORE any simulation work. This is a CRITICAL GATE - if PMU overhead exceeds 3%, the approach may not be viable.

steps.step1: Check CPU features for PMU support: grep -E 'pebs|ibs|arch_perfmon' /proc/cpuinfo | head -20
steps.step2: Check kernel version: uname -r (document: available 6.8.0 vs required 6.12+ for sched_ext)
steps.step3: Install required packages: pip install numpy scipy pandas matplotlib simpy tabulate
steps.step4: Verify perf tool availability: which perf && perf --version
steps.step6: Document hardware specs: lscpu, numactl --hardware (if available), free -h

success_criteria: PMU features available; perf tool functional; environment ready

title: PMU Overhead Validation - CRITICAL GATE
description: Measure actual PMU profiling overhead on real hardware using STREAM benchmark. This experiment determines if the UniSched approach is viable. If overhead > 3%, document and reconsider approach.

steps.step1: Create STREAM benchmark wrapper: Python script to run STREAM with varying array sizes (10M, 50M, 100M elements)
steps.step2: Run baseline STREAM without PMU: ./stream.exe, capture bandwidth (GB/s), record 5 runs
steps.step3: Run STREAM with PEBS at 0.5% sampling: perf record -e mem-loads:u,mem-stores:u -c 200000 -- ./stream.exe
steps.step4: Run STREAM with PEBS at 1.0% sampling: perf record -e mem-loads:u,mem-stores:u -c 100000 -- ./stream.exe
steps.step5: Run STREAM with PEBS at 2.0% sampling: perf record -e mem-loads:u,mem-stores:u -c 50000 -- ./stream.exe
steps.step7: Gate decision: If overhead <= 3%, proceed with confidence. If 3-5%, proceed with documented caution. If >5%, stop and document limitations.
steps.step8: Run additional microbenchmark: RandomAccess (pointer chasing) with/without PMU to validate latency-sensitive workload overhead

success_criteria: PMU overhead <= 5% (ideally <= 3%); results documented with statistical confidence intervals
feedback_addressed: Feedback #3: Validates PMU overhead early before full experiments

title: Topology Discovery and Simulator Calibration
description: Characterize actual hardware NUMA topology and memory latency/bandwidth to calibrate simulator parameters. Even with simulation, we use real measurements where possible.

steps.step1: Enumerate NUMA topology: Create script to parse /sys/devices/system/node/node*/ for memory and CPU assignments
steps.step2: Measure memory latency between NUMA nodes: Use simple pointer-chasing benchmark (if root access available) or use documented typical values
steps.step3: Measure memory bandwidth: STREAM results from PMU validation provide local DRAM bandwidth baseline
steps.step4: Create CXL latency/bandwidth parameters for simulation: DRAM=100ns, CXL-local=200ns, CXL-remote=350ns (based on CXL 2.0/3.0 specifications)
steps.step5: Build topology configuration JSON: {nodes: [{id: 0, cpus: [0-31], memory_gb: 128, type: 'local_dram'}, {id: 1, cpus: [32-63], memory_gb: 512, type: 'cxl_attached'}]}
steps.step6: Create calibration report: Document which parameters are from real measurements vs. CXL specification estimates

success_criteria: Topology documented; simulator parameters calibrated with real measurements where possible; CXL parameters justified with citations

title: Workload Characterization and Dataset Generation
description: Generate synthetic workload traces with varying memory access patterns that will be used across all schedulers for fair comparison. Characteristics based on real application behavior.

steps.step1: Define workload types: (a) Local-dominant (80% local DRAM access), (b) CXL-bandwidth-bound (60% CXL access, sequential), (c) Latency-sensitive (random access, low cache hit), (d) Mixed (combination)
steps.step2: Generate Redis/YCSB-like workload: Key-value operations with Zipfian key distribution, 70% read, read-heavy on hot keys
steps.step3: Generate Graph analytics workload: PageRank-style access patterns - iterative, high memory footprint, good locality
steps.step4: Generate STREAM-like workload: Sequential memory access, bandwidth-bound, configurable read/write ratio (2:1, 1:1, 1:2)
steps.step5: Generate Latency-sensitive workload: Pointer chasing, random access, small working set that fits cache
steps.step6: Scale generation: Create 3 workload sizes - small (10 tasks), medium (50 tasks), large (100 tasks) for scalability testing
steps.step7: Save workload traces as JSON: {workload_id, tasks: [{arrival_time, duration, mem_access_pattern, footprint_mb, access_nodes: {0: 0.8, 1: 0.2}}]}

success_criteria: 5 workload types generated; 3 sizes each; workloads have realistic memory access patterns based on published characteristics

title: Baseline Scheduler 1: Linux EEVDF Simulation
description: Implement simulation of Linux's default EEVDF (Earliest Eligible Virtual Deadline First) scheduler. This is the simplest baseline representing stock Linux without NUMA awareness.

steps.step1: Implement EEVDF scheduler simulator: Virtual time-based scheduling, lag-based fairness, no NUMA awareness
steps.step2: Core algorithm: tasks sorted by virtual deadline, min_vruntime tracking, slice calculation based on weight
steps.step3: NUMA behavior: Tasks can run on any CPU; memory access latency depends on which node task runs on vs where memory was allocated
steps.step4: Run EEVDF on all 5 workload types (small, medium, large) × 3 random seeds (1, 2, 3)
steps.step5: Collect metrics: throughput (tasks completed/sec), avg/tail latency, CPU utilization, memory bandwidth per node

success_criteria: EEVDF baseline implemented and run on all workloads; results stored for comparison

title: Baseline Scheduler 2: EEVDF + AutoNUMA Simulation
description: Implement simulation of Linux EEVDF with AutoNUMA-style reactive page migration. This is the CRITICAL STRONG BASELINE - represents production Linux NUMA-aware scheduling.

steps.step1: Implement AutoNUMA-style reactive migration: Periodic scanning (every 1 second simulated time), page access sampling, CPU-follows-memory / memory-follows-CPU policies
steps.step2: Model AutoNUMA parameters: scan_delay=1s, max_numa_migrate_rate=8MB/s, numa_balancing_scan_size_mb=256
steps.step3: Implement page placement logic: Track where pages are allocated; when task accesses remote memory, trigger migration with bandwidth-limited rate
steps.step4: Model migration overhead: 1 microsecond per page + bandwidth constraint; cache invalidation modeled as cold-start penalty
steps.step5: Run EEVDF+AutoNUMA on all 5 workload types × 3 sizes × 3 random seeds
steps.step6: Collect additional AutoNUMA-specific metrics: migration count, bytes migrated, scan overhead

success_criteria: AutoNUMA baseline with reactive migration implemented; comparison shows expected behavior (some improvement over EEVDF for NUMA workloads)
feedback_addressed: Feedback #2: Add AutoNUMA as baseline in addition to EEVDF; Feedback #5: AutoNUMA is relevant existing mechanism for NUMA-aware scheduling

title: Baseline Scheduler 3: Tiresias-like Reactive Scheduler
description: Implement simulation of Tiresias-style reactive page-fault-driven scheduling. Represents state-of-the-art CXL-aware kernel-modified approach.

steps.step1: Implement Tiresias-like reactive scheduler: Page-fault-driven thread migration, no proactive profiling
steps.step2: Model PTSR behavior: Reduced migration cost for page-table replication (50% of normal migration cost)
steps.step3: Migration trigger: Migrate task to memory-access-dominant NUMA node when remote access ratio exceeds threshold (60%)
steps.step4: Add hysteresis: Minimum interval between migrations (100ms) to prevent thrashing
steps.step5: Run on all 5 workload types × 3 sizes × 3 random seeds
steps.step6: Collect metrics: migration frequency, page faults, throughput, latency

success_criteria: Tiresias-like baseline implemented; shows reactive migration behavior; results comparable to expected Tiresias behavior from literature

title: Baseline Scheduler 4: CXLAimPod-style Intra-Node Optimizer
description: Implement simulation of CXLAimPod-style intra-node duplex optimization. Used to demonstrate the COMPLEMENTARY nature of UniSched (cross-NUMA) vs CXLAimPod (within-node).

steps.step1: Implement CXLAimPod-style scheduler: Within single NUMA node, balance read-heavy and write-heavy tasks on same CXL memory controller
steps.step2: Model full-duplex CXL: Simultaneous read+write bandwidth = 2× unidirectional bandwidth
steps.step3: Task classification: Tag tasks as read-heavy (>70% reads), write-heavy (>70% writes), or balanced
steps.step4: Co-scheduling policy: Pair read-heavy with write-heavy on same node to exploit duplex channels
steps.step5: Note limitation: CXLAimPod doesn't solve cross-NUMA placement - assumes placement already decided
steps.step6: Run on bandwidth-heavy workloads with 2:1, 1:1, 1:2 read/write ratios × 3 seeds

success_criteria: CXLAimPod baseline demonstrates intra-node duplex optimization; results show complementary behavior to cross-NUMA scheduling
feedback_addressed: Feedback #4: Clarify CXLAimPod distinction; Feedback #5: CXLAimPod vs UniSched are at different levels

title: UniSched Implementation - Full System
description: Implement full UniSched simulator with proactive PMU-based profiling, topology-aware scoring, and cross-NUMA placement decisions.

steps.step1: Implement PMU sampling simulator: Sample task memory access at configurable frequency (0.5%, 1%, 2%), track access distribution per NUMA node
steps.step2: Implement task classification: local-dominant (>60% local access), cxl-bandwidth-bound (>40% CXL sequential), latency-sensitive (high random access rate)
steps.step3: Implement topology-aware scoring function: score(cpu, task) = w1*compute_score + w2*memory_score + w3*migration_cost
steps.step4: Set weights: w1=0.4 (compute), w2=0.4 (memory), w3=0.2 (migration); compute_score based on CPU load, memory_score based on access pattern match
steps.step5: Implement latency-aware placement: For local-dominant tasks, strongly prefer same-node CPUs (memory_score bonus)
steps.step6: Implement bandwidth balancing: Monitor CXL controller saturation; migrate bandwidth-heavy tasks to less loaded controllers
steps.step7: Model PMU overhead: Add 0.5-2% overhead (based on Phase 1 validation) to each scheduling decision
steps.step8: Run UniSched on all 5 workload types × 3 sizes × 3 random seeds

success_criteria: UniSched fully implemented with all components; runs on all workloads; PMU overhead modeled based on real measurements

title: Ablation Study 1: Topology-Only UniSched
description: Remove per-task profiling, use only aggregate topology information. Tests the value of proactive PMU-based profiling vs. static topology awareness.

steps.step1: Modify UniSched: Remove per-task memory access tracking; use only NUMA topology for placement
steps.step2: Placement policy: Simple heuristic - prefer local DRAM node for all tasks unless load imbalance exceeds threshold
steps.step3: Run topology-only variant on all 5 workloads × 3 sizes × 3 seeds
steps.step4: Compare against full UniSched to measure value of profiling

success_criteria: Ablation shows topology-only provides some benefit; but full system with profiling does better

title: Ablation Study 2: Profiling-Only UniSched
description: Remove topology-aware scoring, use only per-task profiling with standard load balancing. Tests the value of CXL topology awareness.

steps.step1: Modify UniSched: Keep per-task profiling but use standard load balancing (no memory topology in scoring)
steps.step2: Profiling used only for: Task classification (local vs CXL-bound) for post-hoc analysis, not for scheduling
steps.step3: Run profiling-only variant on all 5 workloads × 3 sizes × 3 seeds
steps.step4: Compare against full UniSched to measure value of topology awareness

success_criteria: Ablation shows profiling alone helps; but combining with topology awareness provides best results

title: Ablation Study 3: No Memory Coordination
description: Remove the memory coordination component (userspace move_pages hints). Tests the value of coordinating CPU scheduling with page migration.

steps.step1: Modify UniSched: Disable memory coordination hints; CPU scheduler works in isolation
steps.step2: Compare with full system that coordinates via simulated move_pages()
steps.step3: Run on workloads with large memory footprints (graph analytics, large STREAM)

success_criteria: Ablation shows memory coordination provides measurable benefit for large-footprint workloads

title: Sensitivity Analysis: PMU Sampling Frequency
description: Test different PMU sampling frequencies to understand the overhead-accuracy tradeoff. Validates that our chosen 1% frequency is appropriate.

steps.step2: For each frequency: Measure scheduling quality (throughput, latency) and overhead
steps.step3: Plot: X-axis=sampling frequency, Y-axis=(performance, overhead); identify knee point
steps.step4: Expected: Diminishing returns above 1-2%; validate that 1% is near-optimal

success_criteria: Sensitivity analysis shows clear tradeoff curve; validates chosen 1% sampling frequency

title: Sensitivity Analysis: CXL Latency Variations
description: Test UniSched performance under different CXL latency configurations to validate robustness across different CXL implementations.

steps.step1: Test CXL latency scenarios: (a) Low latency (CXL local=150ns, remote=250ns), (b) Medium (200ns, 350ns), (c) High (300ns, 500ns)
steps.step2: Run all schedulers (EEVDF, AutoNUMA, Tiresias, UniSched) on each scenario
steps.step3: Measure: Does UniSched's advantage increase with higher CXL latency? (Expected: yes)

success_criteria: UniSched shows consistent improvements across C latency scenarios; advantage scales with CXL latency as expected

title: Statistical Analysis and Hypothesis Testing
description: Perform rigorous statistical analysis on all results. Test the hypothesis that UniSched achieves >=5% improvement over EEVDF+AutoNUMA.

steps.step1: Aggregate all results: Load JSON results from all experiments into pandas DataFrame
steps.step2: Calculate mean and standard deviation for each metric across 3 random seeds
steps.step3: Perform paired t-test: UniSched vs each baseline on each workload; report p-values
steps.step4: Calculate confidence intervals: 95% CI for performance improvements
steps.step5: Compute effect sizes: Cohen's d for practical significance
steps.step6: Fairness analysis: Calculate Jain's fairness index for CPU time distribution across tasks
steps.step7: Test hypothesis: Check if UniSched achieves >=5% throughput improvement on >=3 benchmarks
steps.step8: Test overhead constraint: Verify simulated PMU overhead aligns with Phase 1 validation

success_criteria: Statistical tests complete; p-values reported; confidence intervals calculated; hypothesis tested with evidence

title: Simulation Limitations Documentation
description: Explicitly document all simulation limitations as required by feedback. This is critical for scientific integrity.

steps.step1: Document CXL protocol limitations: Simulation uses parameterized latency/bandwidth; cannot capture actual CXL protocol handshaking, cache coherency overheads, or real contention arbitration
steps.step2: Document sched_ext limitations: Simulator approximates BPF scheduler behavior; actual sched_ext may have different latency characteristics, BPF verifier constraints not captured
steps.step3: Document migration cost limitations: Cache warmth and TLB effects are modeled heuristically based on literature values, not measured
steps.step4: Document PMU simulation limitations: Real PEBS/IBS has skid, latency, and buffer overflow effects not fully modeled
steps.step5: Document kernel version limitation: Results are from simulation, not real sched_ext deployment on Linux 6.12+
steps.step7: Propose validation path: Describe how results could be validated with kernel 6.12+ upgrade

success_criteria: All limitations explicitly documented; mitigation strategies described; path to real-hardware validation outlined
feedback_addressed: Feedback #1: Address simulation limitations more explicitly; Feedback #4: Consider kernel upgrade for real sched_ext validation

title: Generate Performance Comparison Figures
description: Create publication-quality figures comparing UniSched against all baselines. Include error bars and statistical significance markers.

steps.step1: Figure 1 - Throughput Comparison: Bar chart with error bars; X-axis=workloads, Y-axis=throughput (normalized to EEVDF), grouped by scheduler; include statistical significance markers (* for p<0.05, ** for p<0.01)
steps.step2: Figure 2 - Tail Latency Comparison: Similar format for p95 and p99 latency
steps.step3: Figure 3 - Ablation Study: Stacked bar or grouped bar showing contribution of each component (topology-only, profiling-only, full system)
steps.step4: Figure 4 - Sensitivity Analysis: Line plot showing performance vs sampling frequency with shaded confidence intervals
steps.step5: Figure 5 - CXL Bandwidth Utilization: Show improved bandwidth distribution across CXL controllers with UniSched
steps.step6: Figure 6 - Fairness vs Performance Tradeoff: Scatter plot showing Jain's fairness index vs throughput improvement
steps.step7: Save all figures: experiments/results/figures/*.png with 300 DPI for publication

success_criteria: 6 publication-quality figures generated; all include error bars; appropriate statistical markers

title: Generate Summary Tables for Paper
description: Create LaTeX/Markdown tables summarizing all results for inclusion in the paper.

steps.step1: Table 1 - Main Results: Workload | EEVDF | AutoNUMA | Tiresias | UniSched | Improvement over AutoNUMA; include throughput, p95 latency, fairness index
steps.step2: Table 2 - Ablation Study: Component | Throughput | Migration Count | Notes
steps.step3: Table 3 - PMU Overhead Validation: Sampling Frequency | STREAM Bandwidth | Overhead % | Status (validated in Phase 1)
steps.step4: Table 4 - Simulation Parameters: Parameter | Value | Source (measured vs estimated)
steps.step5: Table 5 - Workload Characteristics: Workload | Access Pattern | Local/CXL Ratio | Memory Footprint

success_criteria: All tables generated; properly formatted; include all necessary information for paper

title: Final Success Criteria Evaluation
description: Evaluate all success criteria from the hypothesis and determine if they are met. Document confirming and refuting evidence.

steps.step1: Criterion 1 - PMU Overhead: Check Phase 1 results; document if overhead <= 3%
steps.step2: Criterion 2 - Performance Improvement: Check if >=5% throughput improvement on >=3 benchmarks vs EEVDF+AutoNUMA
steps.step3: Criterion 3 - Bandwidth Utilization: Check if >=10% improvement in CXL bandwidth utilization
steps.step4: Criterion 4 - Fairness: Check if Jain's index remains >= 0.85 under competitive workloads
steps.step5: Criterion 5 - Deployability: Verify scheduler can be implemented via sched_ext (code analysis)
steps.step6: Generate final report: experiments/results/SUCCESS_CRITERIA_EVALUATION.md with PASS/FAIL for each criterion
steps.step7: Document any refuting evidence: If criteria not met, document why and what this means for the hypothesis

success_criteria: All success criteria evaluated; honest assessment of hypothesis support; refuting evidence documented if present
